body:
**Constraints:**
- Use only two sub-structures.

**Goal:**
- Build a single rocket engine capable of providing propulsion to a single direction.
- Build a simple chassis to assemble the rocket engines using braces to form a symmetric rocket.
- The assembled rocket should be able to lift off from the ground to the sky in the simulation environment.

**Evaluation Protocol:**
- The assembled rocket will be placed at position (x=0, y=0, z=0) on the ground plane.
- During the simulation, the firing control key of the rocket engine will be pressed and held continuously.
- The motion trajectory of the assembled rocket will be recorded throughout the simulation.

**Scoring Metrics:**
- *Maximum Height:* The highest vertical position (z) reached by any block of the assembled rocket (higher is better).
- *Trajectory Deviation:* The average lateral distance between the assembled rocket’s actual trajectory and the ideal vertical line (smaller is better).
- *Maximum Speed:* The highest speed achieved by any block of the assembled rocket (higher is better).
- *Cost:* The total number of blocks used to construct the assembled rocket (fewer is better).

title: planner message
summary: <building_plan> <overall_structure> <description> The rocket is a rigid, symmetric assembly made from two identical steam rocket engine modules mounted on opposite sides of a light central chassis spine using solid brac…
body:
<building_plan>
  <overall_structure>
    <description>
      The rocket is a rigid, symmetric assembly made from two identical steam rocket engine modules mounted on opposite sides of a light central chassis spine using solid braces. Each engine module is a downward-pointing Water Cannon that is locally heated by a Torch to run in steam mode (8.6× thrust), mounted under a small wooden mount block. Two small wooden “lug” blocks on the north and south sides of each engine provide brace anchor faces. The central chassis is a simple two-block vertical stack above its own base stone that offers east and west faces at two heights for bracing. Four Braces (two per engine, at two different heights) tie the engines to the chassis to hold the engines vertical and centered, producing a symmetric, vertical-thrust rocket.
    </description>
    <functionality>
      - Propulsion: Each Water Cannon points straight down and, when heated by its Torch, produces steam-mode thrust. Two engines fire simultaneously to produce a strong, symmetric upward force.
      - Stability: Two braces per engine (upper and lower) form rigid triangles to the chassis, resisting pitch/roll and keeping thrust vectors aligned with +z (up).
      - Lift-off feasibility: Each engine provides 1.6 units (water) × 8.6 ≈ 13.76 mass-equivalent thrust in steam mode. Two engines yield ≈27.52. Estimated assembled mass ≈10.15 (see breakdown in sub-structures and connectors), producing initial net upward acceleration >1 g, ensuring lift-off and high maximum height/speed with low lateral deviation due to symmetry.
    </functionality>
    <assembly>
      - Instantiate two copies of Sub-Structure 1 (Steam Rocket Engine Module): Engine-L (west side) and Engine-R (east side).
      - Build one copy of Sub-Structure 2 (Central Chassis Spine) at the origin.
      - Spatial arrangement (plan view): Place Engine-L to the west (negative x) of the chassis and Engine-R to the east (positive x), at the same z level so their mount blocks are aligned with the chassis stack. Keep several block-widths of lateral clearance so torches and cannons do not intersect the chassis; braces will span the gap.
      - Bracing (4 total):
        • Engine-L: connect a Brace from the outward face of its north lug to the west face of the upper chassis block; connect a second Brace from the outward face of its south lug to the west face of the lower chassis block.
        • Engine-R: connect a Brace from the outward face of its north lug to the east face of the upper chassis block; connect a second Brace from the outward face of its south lug to the east face of the lower chassis block.
      - Ensure all braces are taut and of similar length left/right to preserve symmetry. Maintain enough lateral spacing so the torches sit clear of the chassis and each other, and so cannon outlets are a small distance above ground at rest to avoid clipping.
    </assembly>
    <motion_control>
      - Configure both Water Cannons (Engine-L and Engine-R) to the same firing control key (e.g., “F”). The evaluation will hold this key continuously.
      - Torches are passive and remain lit; no control needed.
      - Expected motion: Upon firing, torches heat the cannon bodies into steam mode within the small spherical heating area (radius 0.3) adjacent to the lower side of each cannon. Thrust vectors are vertical and balanced, so the rocket rises approximately straight up with minimal lateral deviation. Two-height bracing suppresses pitch/yaw/roll.
    </motion_control>
  </overall_structure>

  <sub_structures>
    <sub_structure_1>
      <name>Steam Rocket Engine Module</name>
      <description>
        A compact, self-contained steam rocket engine that generates vertical thrust. It consists of a small wooden mount block attached to its base stone, a Water Cannon mounted to the mount’s bottom face pointing downward, a Torch on the mount’s side pointing downward to heat the cannon into steam mode, and two opposite small wooden lug blocks (north and south) used solely as brace anchor points to connect to the chassis.
      </description>
      <components>
        - 1x Default Stone Base (1×1×1, mass 0.25)
        - 1x Small Wooden Block (mount, mass 0.3)
        - 1x Water Cannon (mass 1.5)
        - 1x Torch (mass 1.0)
        - 2x Small Wooden Block (brace lugs: north and south, each mass 0.3)
      </components>
      <assembly>
        - Start from the sub-structure’s default stone base.
        - Attach the mount Small Wooden Block (Wm) to the TOP face of the base stone (centers aligned).
        - Attach the Water Cannon to the BOTTOM face of Wm, outlet pointing straight DOWN (−z). This orients thrust upward (+z).
        - Attach a Torch to the EAST face of Wm, oriented DOWNWARD. The torch’s horizontal support offsets ~0.5 out from Wm’s east face; its heating sphere (radius 0.3) is centered ~1.0 below the torch body. This places the heating sphere adjacent to the lower side of the Water Cannon body (clear of the direct exhaust axis), reliably heating it to steam mode.
        - Attach two Small Wooden Blocks as brace lugs: one to the NORTH face of Wm (lug-N) and one to the SOUTH face of Wm (lug-S). Their outward faces (north of lug-N and south of lug-S) will be kept free for Braces during final assembly.
        - Face use tally (to avoid conflicts): Wm top used by base; bottom used by cannon; east used by torch; north and south used by lugs; west remains free.
        - Clearance: Ensure the Water Cannon hangs below Wm with its outlet below Wm but slightly above the ground upon final assembly; the Torch sits lateral to the cannon, not directly under the outlet.
      </assembly>
      <motion_control>
        - Assign the Water Cannon to the shared firing key (e.g., “F”).
        - Torch is always on (no control).
        - Behavior: Holding the key runs the cannon; torch heating transitions output to steam mode (≈8.6× thrust), producing strong upward thrust.
      </motion_control>
      <function>
        Provides one vertical steam rocket engine with integral heating and brace anchors. Two instances are used to create a symmetric propulsion pair for the final rocket.
      </function>
      <design_requirements>
        - The Water Cannon must point straight down with its axis aligned to global z.
        - The Torch’s heating sphere (radius 0.3) must intersect the Water Cannon’s lower side body (avoid the narrow waist); place the torch slightly lateral and below the mount so it is not directly in the exhaust stream to reduce extinguishing risk.
        - Keep lug-N and lug-S outward faces free for Braces.
        - Ensure ground clearance for the cannon outlet at rest; adjust engine-to-chassis spacing via brace lengths as needed.
        - Positions may be micro‑adjusted in later stages to resolve conflicts based on actual build execution.
      </design_requirements>
    </sub_structure_1>

    <sub_structure_2>
      <name>Central Chassis Spine</name>
      <description>
        A minimal, lightweight vertical spine that provides two bracing faces per side (east and west) at two different heights. It serves as the central mass and rigid anchor for the two engine modules.
      </description>
      <components>
        - 1x Default Stone Base (1×1×1, mass 0.25)
        - 2x Small Wooden Block (stacked vertically above the base, each mass 0.3)
      </components>
      <assembly>
        - Attach the first Small Wooden Block (C0) to the TOP face of the chassis base stone.
        - Attach the second Small Wooden Block (C1) to the TOP face of C0 (stacked).
        - Keep the EAST and WEST faces of both C0 and C1 free for Braces. This yields two bracing heights per side.
        - The NORTH/SOUTH faces remain open for future extensions if needed but are unused in this plan.
      </assembly>
      <motion_control>
        - None. The chassis is passive.
      </motion_control>
      <function>
        Provides symmetric, rigid mounting points to tie in the two engine modules with triangular bracing, keeping thrust vectors vertical and aligned through the rocket’s centerline for stable ascent.
      </function>
      <design_requirements>
        - Maintain both EAST and WEST faces of C0 (lower) and C1 (upper) unoccupied to accept Braces from engines.
        - Position the chassis between the two engines with roughly equal lateral spacing left/right; brace lengths should be similar for symmetry.
        - Ensure at-rest ground clearance so engine outlets are just above the ground plane after assembly.
        - Positions may be micro‑adjusted in later stages to resolve conflicts based on actual build execution.
      </design_requirements>
    </sub_structure_2>
  </sub_structures>
</building_plan>

Block 1 - Small Wooden Block - lower spine segment C0; reserve EAST/WEST faces for future Braces - TOP face of Block 0

Block 2 - Small Wooden Block - upper spine segment C1; reserve EAST/WEST faces for future Braces - TOP face of Block 1

Supplementary placement and design notes:
- Coordinates for reference (assuming Block 0 center at [0, 0, 0.5]):
  - Block 1 (C0) center at [0, 0, 1.5]; EAST face center [0.5, 0, 1.5], WEST face center [-0.5, 0, 1.5]
  - Block 2 (C1) center at [0, 0, 2.5]; EAST face center [0.5, 0, 2.5], WEST face center [-0.5, 0, 2.5]
- Faces reserved:

  - NORTH and SOUTH faces of all three blocks remain open for potential future extensions but are unused in this sub-structure.
- Symmetry guidance for later assembly:
  - Position the chassis roughly centered between the two engine modules so brace lengths to EAST and WEST are similar.
  - The lower bracing height (C0 at z≈1.5) and upper bracing height (C1 at z≈2.5) provide two triangles per side for rigidity.

summary: Step 1: Establish coordinate system and base reference - Axes: x = East/West, y = North/South, z = Up. - Block dimensions: Small Wooden Block is 1×1×1 (width×depth×height). - Starting block (Block 0, Default Base) is 1×…
body:
Step 1: Establish coordinate system and base reference
- Axes: x = East/West, y = North/South, z = Up.
- Block dimensions: Small Wooden Block is 1×1×1 (width×depth×height).
- Starting block (Block 0, Default Base) is 1×1×1 with center at [0, 0, 0.5] and bottom at z = 0 as stated by the drafter. This is acceptable and consistent with the default base requirement.

Step 2: Structural integrity and collision checks, block-by-block
For each new block, position is relative to Block 0, then distances to neighboring blocks are checked against minimum separations (sum of half-dimensions along the axis of separation; for cubes of size 1, the half-dimension is 0.5 per axis, so threshold per axis is 1).

Block 1 (Small Wooden Block, C0)
- Placement: Attached to TOP face of Block 0.
- Exact center coordinates: [0, 0, 1.5].
- Neighboring blocks: Block 0 (below), Block 2 (above).
- Distances to neighboring centers (Euclidean):
  - To Block 0: distance = sqrt((0−0)^2 + (0−0)^2 + (1.5−0.5)^2) = 1.0
  - To Block 2: distance = sqrt((0−0)^2 + (0−0)^2 + (1.5−2.5)^2) = 1.0
- Axis separation checks:
  - Versus Block 0: Δz = 1.0; required minimum along z = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0 → exactly flush, no overlap. Δx = 0, Δy = 0 (<1), but separation along z meets the threshold, so contact without overlap is valid.
  - Versus Block 2: Δz = 1.0; required minimum along z = 1.0 → exactly flush, no overlap.
- Reserved faces:
  - EAST face center [0.5, 0, 1.5] and WEST face center [-0.5, 0, 1.5] are unoccupied.
  - NORTH/SOUTH faces are open and unused in this sub-structure.
- Attachment correctness: Cube-on-cube top-face attachment yields center rise of +1 in z, consistent with the given coordinates. No improper attachment.

Block 2 (Small Wooden Block, C1)
- Placement: Attached to TOP face of Block 1.
- Exact center coordinates: [0, 0, 2.5].
- Neighboring blocks: Block 1 (below), Block 0 (two below).
- Distances to neighboring centers (Euclidean):
  - To Block 1: distance = sqrt((0−0)^2 + (0−0)^2 + (2.5−1.5)^2) = 1.0
  - To Block 0: distance = sqrt((0−0)^2 + (0−0)^2 + (2.5−0.5)^2) = 2.0
- Axis separation checks:
  - Versus Block 1: Δz = 1.0; required minimum along z = 1.0 → exactly flush, no overlap.
  - Versus Block 0: Δz = 2.0; required minimum along z = 1.0 → clear separation, no overlap.
- Reserved faces:
  - EAST face center [0.5, 0, 2.5] and WEST face center [-0.5, 0, 2.5] are unoccupied.
  - NORTH/SOUTH faces remain open.
- Attachment correctness: Proper stacked placement on top face. No improper attachment.

Overlap and attachment summary
- No overlaps detected. All stacked attachments are face-to-face with Δz = 1.0 between consecutive blocks, which equals the minimum required separation for unit cubes (flush contact).
- All reserved faces (EAST/WEST on C0 and C1) remain unoccupied, satisfying the design requirement.

Step 3: Functional validation
- Motion control: None; the chassis is passive. No moving components present.
- Functional intent:
  - Provides two bracing heights per side (EAST/WEST on C0 at z=1.5 and C1 at z=2.5). This is satisfied by the unoccupied faces and their positions.
  - Symmetry and centerline: The spine is centered at x=0, y=0, enabling symmetric brace lengths to EAST and WEST when engines are added. This supports later triangular bracing and vertical thrust alignment.
  - Future integration: NORTH/SOUTH faces left open as specified. No conflicts with current plan.
- Missing/contingent items:
  - Engine module placement and brace geometry are not yet defined, so exact brace lengths and ground clearance for engine outlets cannot be fully confirmed at this stage. However, the provided spine heights are compatible with typical brace spans and the ground plane is clear beneath the base.

Step 4: Physical feasibility assessment
- Mass and rigidity:
  - Block 0 mass 0.25; Block 1 mass 0.3; Block 2 mass 0.3; total sub-structure mass = 0.85. A stacked wooden spine is feasible and rigid enough for bracing with braces added later.
- Ground clearance:
  - Base bottom at z=0; no elements extend below ground. With spine rising to z=3 (top of C1 is at z=3), there is ample vertical range to brace engine modules such that their outlets can be located just above z=0; final clearance depends on engine module geometry, to be validated later.
- Attachment points:
  - EAST/WEST faces at two heights provide the necessary anchors for triangular bracing per side. The faces are unobstructed and accessible.

Conclusion
- Structural integrity: Pass. Positions are precise, attachments are correct, and no overlaps exist.
- Functional implementation: Pass for this sub-structure; it provides the intended bracing faces and central spine. Future elements (engines/braces) will determine final symmetry and ground clearance.
- Physical feasibility: Pass. The spine is minimal, centered, and suitable for later engine bracing.

TERMINATE

summary: Sub-structure name: Steam Rocket Engine Module Blocks declaration and placement (built from the default 1×1×1 starting base): - Block 0 - Default Starting Block (1×1×1 base stone) - base reference for the module - origi…
body:
Sub-structure name: Steam Rocket Engine Module

Blocks declaration and placement (built from the default 1×1×1 starting base):
- Block 0 - Default Starting Block (1×1×1 base stone) - base reference for the module - origin reference
- Block 1 - Small Wooden Block - mount (Wm), 1×1×1, structural hub for attachments - top face of Block 0
- Block 2 - Water Cannon - vertical thrust cannon mounted under Wm - bottom face of Block 1 - points straight down (global −z). Assigned to shared firing key “F”. When held, the cannon fires; with nearby torch heating, it transitions to steam mode (~8.6× recoil), producing strong upward (+z) thrust.
- Block 3 - Torch - side heater for steam mode, oriented downward - east face of Block 1 - always on. Orientation vector downward (global −z). Heating sphere radius 0.3 centered approximately one unit below the torch body (east of the cannon axis), intersecting the lower side of the Water Cannon’s body to reliably heat it while remaining lateral to the exhaust stream.
- Block 4 - Small Wooden Block - brace lug-N (anchor for future Brace connection), keep outward NORTH face free - north face of Block 1
- Block 5 - Small Wooden Block - brace lug-S (anchor for future Brace connection), keep outward SOUTH face free - south face of Block 1

Functional geometry and behavior details:
- Global axes: +x east, +y north, +z upward.
- Water Cannon placement and geometry: attached to Wm’s bottom face; axis aligned with global z and pointing −z. The cannon body hangs below Wm; outlet is below the mount, ensuring vertical thrust upward. Recoil is constant (1.6 at normal gravity) and rises to ~13.76 in steam mode (8.6×).
- Torch placement and heating: attached to Wm’s east face, horizontal support offsets ~0.5 unit east from the face. Torch points downward; its heating sphere (r = 0.3) is centered ~1.0 unit below the torch body, situated east of the cannon axis and at the lower-half height of the cannon. This positioning causes the sphere to intersect the cannon’s lower side body (avoiding the narrow waist and avoiding direct alignment under the outlet), reliably triggering steam mode while minimizing extinguishing risk from the downward plume.

Control assignments:
- Water Cannon: key “F” (shared firing key). Hold to fire.
- Torch: always on (no control).

Face-use tally for Wm (Block 1):
- Top: used by Block 0 (base).
- Bottom: used by Block 2 (Water Cannon).
- East: used by Block 3 (Torch).
- North: used by Block 4 (lug-N).
- South: used by Block 5 (lug-S).
- West: remains free (available for future needs).

Clearance notes:
- The Water Cannon outlet sits below Wm while remaining above expected ground clearance in typical chassis mounting; final ground clearance is adjusted via Brace lengths when integrating into the rocket chassis.
- Lugs’ outward faces (north of Block 4 and south of Block 5) are intentionally kept free for Braces during final assembly.

Module function:
- Provides one compact, self-contained vertical steam rocket engine with integral heating and brace anchors. Use two identical modules for symmetric propulsion in the final rocket.

Position adjustment note:
- None at this stage. If later assembly reveals interference (e.g., steam plume intermittently extinguishing the torch), we will micro-adjust the east-side torch by swapping to the west face (Block 1 west) or by slight chassis brace spacing changes, keeping the torch lateral and its heating sphere intersecting the lower side of the cannon body. Functional intent (downward cannon, downward torch, lug-N/S free faces) remains unchanged.

body:
Step 1 — Establish coordinate system and base reference
- Assume the default base block (Block 0) is a 1×1×1 cube centered at [0, 0, 0].
- Face centers of a 1×1×1 cube:
  - Top face center: [0, 0, +0.5]
  - Bottom face center: [0, 0, −0.5]
  - East face center: [+0.5, 0, 0]
  - West face center: [−0.5, 0, 0]
  - North face center: [0, +0.5, 0]
  - South face center: [0, −0.5, 0]

Available block shapes (half-dimensions for collision checks):
- Small Wooden Block: 1×1×1; half sizes = 0.5 (x), 0.5 (y), 0.5 (z)
- Water Cannon: 1.75×1×1 (length along its axis; “waist” as center, asymmetrical ends: inlet at +0.75 along axis and outlet at −1 along axis). Treat half-widths as: x = 0.5, y = 0.5; along axis use 1.0 (downward side) and 0.75 (upward side). For AABB checks, conservatively use max half-length 1.0 on z.
- Torch: 1.5×0.5×0.5. When attached to a vertical face, the torch body center is offset 0.5 along the face normal; heating sphere center is torch body center plus a unit-length orientation vector. Half-widths: x = 0.25, y = 0.25, z = 0.75 when the long dimension is along z (downward orientation).

Step 2 — Place each block and compute exact center coordinates
Block 0 — Default base stone (given)
- Center: [0, 0, 0]

Block 1 — Small Wooden Block (Wm) attached to Block 0 top face
- Attachment: Wm to top face of Block 0, face-to-face.
- Center: [0, 0, 1.0]

Block 2 — Water Cannon attached to Wm bottom face, pointing straight down (−z)
- Wm bottom face center: [0, 0, 0.5]
- Water Cannon center is offset 0.5 along the face normal (−z): [0, 0, 0.5 − 0.5] = [0, 0, 0.0]
- Orientation: axis aligned with global z and pointing −z.
- Inlet position: center +0.75 along +z = [0, 0, +0.75]
- Outlet position: center −1.0 along −z = [0, 0, −1.0]
- Axial extent (z): from −1.0 to +0.75
- Transverse extents (x,y): ±0.5 about the center.

Block 3 — Torch attached to Wm east face, oriented downward (−z)
- Wm east face center: [+0.5, 0, 1.0]
- Torch body center offset +0.5 along +x: [1.0, 0, 1.0]
- Orientation vector: strictly downward (0, 0, −1)
- Heating sphere radius: 0.3; sphere center = torch body center + (orientation vector) = [1.0, 0, 0.0]

Block 4 — Small Wooden Block lug-N attached to Wm north face
- Wm north face center: [0, +0.5, 1.0]
- lug-N center: [0, +1.0, 1.0]
- Keep lug-N outward NORTH face free (no connections on its north face).

Block 5 — Small Wooden Block lug-S attached to Wm south face
- Wm south face center: [0, −0.5, 1.0]
- lug-S center: [0, −1.0, 1.0]
- Keep lug-S outward SOUTH face free.

Step 3 — Neighbor distances and collision checks for each new block
Method: Report center-to-center vector and Euclidean distance; then per-axis separations vs. sum of half-dimensions to confirm overlap/contact/separation.

Block 1 (Wm) vs. Block 0 (base)
- Center vector: [0, 0, +1]; Euclidean distance = 1.0
- Axis separations: |dx|=0 vs sum_half_x=0.5+0.5=1.0; |dy|=0 vs sum_half_y=1.0; |dz|=1.0 vs sum_half_z=1.0
- Result: No overlap. Faces are flush at z=+0.5 plane (proper attachment).

Block 2 (Water Cannon) vs. Block 1 (Wm)
- Center vector: [0, 0, −1]; distance = 1.0
- Axis separations: |dx|=0 vs 1.0; |dy|=0 vs 1.0; |dz|=1.0 vs sum_half_z=0.5 (Wm) + 1.0 (cannon) = 1.5
- All three axes satisfy |d| < sum_half on x and y, and |dz| < sum_half on z → Overlap in volume.
- Also by geometry, the cannon’s upper extent (+0.75) intrudes 0.25 units above Wm’s bottom face at z=0.5, penetrating into Wm (0.5 to 0.75 region).
- Structural status: Overlap detected. Improper attachment/placement.

Block 2 (Water Cannon) vs. Block 0 (base)
- Center vector: [0, 0, 0]; distance = 0
- Axis separations: |dx|=0 vs 1.0; |dy|=0 vs 1.0; |dz|=0 vs sum_half_z=0.5 (base) + 1.0 (cannon) = 1.5
- Result: Severe overlap (complete overlap in x,y and substantial overlap in z). The cannon occupies the same central volume as the base.

Block 3 (Torch) vs. Block 1 (Wm)
- Center vector: [+1, 0, 0]; distance = 1.0
- Axis separations: |dx|=1.0 vs sum_half_x=0.5 (Wm) + 0.25 (torch) = 0.75 → separated along x; thus no overlap.
- Functional heating proximity: Torch heating sphere center [1, 0, 0] is 1.0 units east of the cannon center; the cannon’s east face is at x=+0.5. The minimal x-gap between the torch sphere and cannon body is 0.2 (sphere leftmost x=1 − 0.3 = 0.7 vs cannon east face x=0.5); therefore the heating sphere does NOT intersect the cannon.

Block 3 (Torch) vs. Block 2 (Water Cannon)
- Center vector: [+1, 0, +1]; distance ≈ 1.414
- Axis separations: x: 1.0 > 0.75 (separated), y: 0 < 0.75 (overlap in y), z: 1.0 < 1.75 (overlap in z by extents). Since there is separation along x, there is no volumetric overlap overall.

Block 4 (lug-N) vs. Block 1 (Wm)
- Center vector: [0, +1, 0]; distance = 1.0
- Axis separations: |dy|=1.0 vs sum_half_y=1.0 → flush contact on y; |dx|=0 < 1.0; |dz|=0 < 1.0
- Result: Proper face attachment; no overlap.

Block 4 (lug-N) vs. Block 0 (base)
- Center vector: [0, +1, +1]; distance ≈ 1.414
- Axis separations: Along y: 1.0 vs 1.0 → tangent at y=+0.5; Along z: 1.0 vs 1.0 → tangent at z=+0.5; Along x: 0 < 1.0; Result: Tangential edge contact only (no volume overlap). Acceptable.

Block 5 (lug-S) vs. Block 1 (Wm)
- Center vector: [0, −1, 0]; distance = 1.0
- Axis separations: |dy|=1.0 vs 1.0 → flush contact on y; |dx|=0 < 1.0; |dz|=0 < 1.0
- Result: Proper face attachment; no overlap.

Block 5 (lug-S) vs. Block 0 (base)
- Center vector: [0, −1, +1]; distance ≈ 1.414
- Axis separations: similar to lug-N vs base; tangential edge contact only. Acceptable.

Step 4 — Functional validation
- Water Cannon direction: Specified to point straight down (axis aligned to global −z). Confirmed by placement and orientation.
- Torch heating: Specified “oriented downward (global −z)” and must have its heating sphere intersect the Water Cannon’s lower side body. With the torch body at [1, 0, 1] and heating sphere center at [1, 0, 0], the sphere radius (0.3) does not reach the cannon body (east face is at x=0.5, sphere’s leftmost x is 0.7). Therefore, the torch fails to heat the cannon; steam mode cannot be guaranteed.
- Face-use tally on Wm:
  - Top: attached to base → used
  - Bottom: used by Water Cannon
  - East: used by Torch
  - North: used by lug-N
  - South: used by lug-S
  - West: free
- Motion/control: Water Cannon on key “F”, Torch always on. Control assignment is consistent.

Step 5 — Physical feasibility assessment
- Key structural failures prevent operation:
  1) The Water Cannon overlaps both Wm and the base block due to the specified geometry and offsets when mounted on Wm’s bottom face. This is an improper attachment and a collision that invalidates the build.
  2) The Torch’s heating sphere does not intersect the Water Cannon’s body when mounted to Wm’s east face with a strictly downward (−z) orientation vector. Steam mode cannot be achieved as designed.
- Without resolving these, the module cannot function as intended (no steam transition; physical collision prevents assembly).
- Mass accounting (for context): Base 0.25 + Wm 0.3 + Cannon 1.5 + Torch 1.0 + lugs 0.3+0.3 = 3.65 mass units. Thrust numbers are irrelevant until structural/heating issues are corrected.

Step 6 — Required corrections
To pass structural integrity and satisfy functional heating, you must revise at least the following:

A) Fix the Water Cannon collision:
- Option A1: Add a spacer block (another 1×1×1 Small Wooden Block) between Wm and the cannon so that the cannon center sits below the base block. For example: Base at [0,0,0], Wm at [0,0,1], Spacer at [0,0,0] between Wm and Cannon won’t work because faces are single-use; instead, place Wm on top of an added spacer above the base: Base [0,0,0], Spacer [0,0,1], Wm [0,0,2], Water Cannon center then at [0,0,1.5] − 0.5 = [0,0,1.0]? You’d need to recompute to ensure the cannon’s entire body lies clear of the base. Practically, you need the cannon center z ≤ −0.5 to avoid any overlap with the base (so its upper extent +0.75 ≤ −0.5, i.e., center z ≤ −1.25), which requires elevating Wm far above the base. This is not achievable with the current single Wm unless you add at least two spacer blocks or remove the base.
- Option A2: Remove the base block from the module once assembly is complete (if the base is only a temporary construction aid). If the base must remain as a structural element per your components list, you cannot mount the cannon to Wm’s bottom face without collision under the current geometry.
- Option A3: Relocate the Water Cannon to a side face (e.g., Wm west face) while keeping its axis aligned to global −z. This avoids the base overlap. Note: you cannot then also mount the torch on the same face; you must choose different faces. Your current specification strictly requires the cannon be on the bottom face, so you would need to change that requirement.

B) Ensure the Torch’s heating sphere intersects the cannon lower-side body:
- With the torch on the Wm east face and a strictly downward orientation vector (0,0,−1), intersection does not occur. You must:
  - Option B1: Tilt the torch’s orientation slightly inward (westward) while generally pointing downward so the heating sphere center moves from [1,0,0] to, for example, [0.7,0,0.05] (orientation vector approximately [−0.3, 0, −0.95], unit length). This places the sphere’s leftmost x at 0.4, which intersects the cannon’s east side (x up to 0.5) at z≈0.05—satisfying the intersection requirement while remaining lateral and below the mount.
  - Option B2: Move the torch to a different face and orientation where the heating sphere reaches the cannon’s lower-side body without being directly under the outlet (e.g., on Wm west face with orientation slightly downward and eastward), provided the face is free. Currently west is free, but be cautious about distances and face occupancy with other components.

C) Keep lug-N and lug-S faces free:
- Current placement of lugs on north and south faces of Wm is fine; their outward faces remain free.

Step 7 — Conclusion
- Structural integrity: FAIL. The Water Cannon overlaps both Wm and the base block in the current placement.
- Functional implementation: FAIL. The Torch’s heating sphere does not intersect the Water Cannon body with a strictly downward orientation; steam mode cannot be achieved.
- Physical feasibility: FAIL due to the above.

Please revise by either:
- Elevating Wm (adding spacer blocks) or removing the base so the Water Cannon can hang completely clear of the base and Wm; and
- Adjusting the torch orientation vector to include a westward component so its heating sphere intersects the cannon’s lower side while remaining lateral to the exhaust.

Once you provide a corrected blueprint with updated coordinates and face use that resolves these collisions and heating geometry, I will re-evaluate.

Sub-structure name: Steam Rocket Engine Module (revised to resolve collisions and ensure reliable steam heating)

Blocks declaration and placement (built from the default 1×1×1 starting base):
- Block 0 - Default Starting Block (1×1×1 base stone) - base reference for the module - origin reference
- Block 1 - Small Wooden Block - mount (Wm), 1×1×1, structural hub for attachments - top face of Block 0
- Block 4 - Small Wooden Block - brace lug-N (anchor for future Brace connection), keep outward NORTH face free - north face of Block 1
- Block 5 - Small Wooden Block - brace lug-S (anchor for future Brace connection), keep outward SOUTH face free - south face of Block 1
- Block 2 - Water Cannon - vertical thrust cannon mounted to Wm’s WEST face - west face of Block 1 - points straight down (global −z). Assigned to shared firing key “F”. Firing produces constant recoil (1.6); with nearby torch heating it transitions to steam mode (~8.6×, ≈13.76 equivalent recoil), giving strong upward (+z) thrust.
- Block 3 - Torch - lateral heater mounted to lug-N’s WEST face, angled mostly downward and slightly southward - west face of Block 4 - always on. Orientation vector = [0, −0.8, −0.6] (unit). Heating sphere radius 0.3 centered at torch body center plus this vector; placed to intersect the cannon’s lower-side body while staying lateral to the exhaust stream.

Global axes reference: +x east, +y north, +z upward.

Exact centers and key geometry (for verification):
- Block 0 (Base) center: [0, 0, 0]
- Block 1 (Wm) center: [0, 0, 1.0] (attached to Block 0 top)
- Block 4 (lug-N) center: [0, +1.0, 1.0] (attached to Wm north)
- Block 5 (lug-S) center: [0, −1.0, 1.0] (attached to Wm south)

Water Cannon (Block 2):
- Host face: Wm west face center at [−0.5, 0, 1.0]
- Cannon body center (0.5 offset along face normal): [−1.0, 0, 1.0]
- Axis: global −z (downward)
- Inlet position (center +0.75 along +z): [−1.0, 0, 1.75]
- Outlet position (center −1.0 along −z): [−1.0, 0, 0.0]
- AABB extents: x ∈ [−1.5, −0.5], y ∈ [−0.5, +0.5], z ∈ [0.0, 1.75]

Torch (Block 3):
- Host face: lug-N west face center at [−0.5, 1.0, 1.0]
- Torch body center (0.5 offset along −x): [−1.0, 1.0, 1.0]
- Orientation vector (unit): v = [0, −0.8, −0.6] (mostly downward, slightly south)
- Heating sphere center: [−1.0, 0.2, 0.4] with radius r = 0.3

Collision and proximity checks (sanity):
- Cannon vs Wm: center-to-center vector [−1, 0, 0]; along x: separation = 1.0 equals sum of half-widths (0.5 + 0.5) → proper flush at the west face; no volume overlap.
- Cannon vs Base: vector [−1, 0, +1]; along x: separation = 1.0 equals 0.5 + 0.5 → tangent at base west plane; no overlap; y and z within summed half-extents.
- Torch body vs lug-N: vector [−1, 0, 0]; x separation 1.0 > 0.5 + 0.25 = 0.75 → clear; no overlap.
- Torch body vs Wm: vector [−1, +1, 0]; x and y separations both 1.0 > 0.75 → clear; no overlap.
- Torch body vs Cannon: vector [0, +1, 0]; y separation 1.0 > 0.5 + 0.25 = 0.75 → clear; no overlap.
- Heating sphere vs Cannon: sphere center [−1.0, 0.2, 0.4] lies laterally 0.2 from cannon axis (x=−1, y=0); with sphere radius 0.3 and cannon half-width 0.5, intersection occurs (0.2 ≤ 0.8), at z = 0.4 (well below the cannon center and above the outlet), satisfying the steam-heating requirement while remaining out of direct exhaust.

Control assignments:
- Water Cannon: key “F” (hold to fire)
- Torch: always on (no control)

Face-use tally:
- Wm (Block 1): Top used by Base; West used by Cannon; North used by lug-N; South used by lug-S; East remains free; Bottom remains free.
- lug-N (Block 4): West used by Torch; North face remains free for brace anchor (as required).
- lug-S (Block 5): South face remains free for brace anchor (as required).

Clearance notes:
- Cannon outlet at z = 0.0 is below Wm but lateral to the base; no ground strike expected in typical chassis mounting. Final ground clearance can be tuned by brace lengths during chassis integration.
- Torch is laterally offset and above the outlet plane (heating sphere center z=0.4, bottom of sphere z≈0.1), minimizing extinguishing risk from the downward plume while maintaining reliable heating contact on the cannon’s lower-side body.

Module function:
- Delivers one compact, self-contained vertical steam rocket engine with integral heating and brace anchors. Use two mirrored instances for a symmetric propulsion pair.

Position adjustment note (to resolve reviewer’s Step 5 failures while preserving functional intent):
- Repositioned Water Cannon from Wm bottom face to Wm west face (axis still straight down, −z). Rationale: With the fixed 0.5 attachment offset and the cannon’s +0.75 inlet extent, mounting to a horizontal bottom face causes unavoidable 0.25 intrusion into the mount (and overlap with the base). Side-face mounting eliminates that geometric conflict while fully preserving the requirement “Water Cannon must point straight down.”
- Moved Torch from Wm east face to lug-N west face and adjusted orientation from strictly downward to v = [0, −0.8, −0.6]. Rationale: Ensures the heating sphere intersects the cannon’s lower-side body (reliable steam mode) while staying lateral to the exhaust and keeping lug-N/S outward faces free for Braces. This change adheres to “Torch on the mount’s side pointing downward” in spirit and satisfies the explicit heating geometry requirement.

Mass accounting (unchanged components):
- Base 0.25 + Wm 0.3 + Cannon 1.5 + Torch 1.0 + lug-N 0.3 + lug-S 0.3 = 3.65 units total.

body:
Review overview
- I re-established a consistent coordinate frame and recalculated all block centers and extents based on your revised placements.
- I then checked, for each placed block, distances to all neighboring blocks’ centers and compared per-axis separations against the sum of half-dimensions to flag overlaps or improper attachments.
- Finally, I validated functional requirements (cannon orientation, torch heating intersection, brace faces left free) and assessed physical feasibility.

Step 1 — Coordinate system and base reference
- Base (Block 0) assumed as a 1×1×1 cube centered at [0, 0, 0].
- Face centers for a 1×1×1 cube:
  - Top: [0, 0, +0.5]
  - Bottom: [0, 0, −0.5]
  - East: [+0.5, 0, 0]
  - West: [−0.5, 0, 0]
  - North: [0, +0.5, 0]
  - South: [0, −0.5, 0]

Block half-sizes for collision checks
- Small Wooden Block: half sizes = 0.5 (x), 0.5 (y), 0.5 (z)
- Water Cannon: peanut cylinder length 1.75 along its axis, width 1, height 1. For AABB checks with axis aligned to global z:
  - Half-sizes: 0.5 (x), 0.5 (y), 1.0 (z)
  - Axial extent relative to center: outlet −1.0 along −z; inlet +0.75 along +z
- Torch: shape 1.5×0.5×0.5; when mounted to a vertical face and oriented mostly downward, treat half-sizes conservatively as 0.25 (x), 0.25 (y), 0.75 (z). Heating sphere radius = 0.3, centered at torch body center plus the unit orientation vector.

Step 2 — Block placements (exact centers)
- Block 0 (Base): [0, 0, 0]
- Block 1 (Wm mount) on Block 0 top face: [0, 0, 1.0]
- Block 4 (lug-N) on Wm north face: [0, +1.0, 1.0]
- Block 5 (lug-S) on Wm south face: [0, −1.0, 1.0]

Block 2 — Water Cannon on Wm west face, axis −z (down)
- Wm west face center: [−0.5, 0, 1.0]
- Cannon center offset 0.5 along face normal (−x): [−1.0, 0, 1.0]
- Axial z extent: min z = 0.0 (outlet), max z = 1.75 (inlet)
- Transverse extents: x ∈ [−1.5, −0.5], y ∈ [−0.5, +0.5]

Block 3 — Torch on lug-N west face, orientation vector v = [0, −0.8, −0.6] (unit)
- lug-N west face center: [−0.5, 1.0, 1.0]
- Torch body center offset 0.5 along face normal (−x): [−1.0, 1.0, 1.0]
- Heating sphere center: torch body center + v = [−1.0, 0.2, 0.4]

Step 3 — Neighbor distances and collision checks
Method: For each new block, compute center-to-center vector and Euclidean distance. Check per-axis separations against sum of half sizes to confirm flush attachment, separation, or overlap.

Block 1 (Wm) vs Block 0 (Base)
- Vector: [0, 0, +1.0]; distance = 1.0
- Axis separations: |dx|=0 ≤ 1.0; |dy|=0 ≤ 1.0; |dz|=1.0 = 1.0 (0.5 + 0.5)
- Result: Proper flush attachment at the top face; no overlap.

Block 4 (lug-N) vs Block 1 (Wm)
- Vector: [0, +1.0, 0]; distance = 1.0
- Axis separations: |dy|=1.0 = 1.0; |dx|=0 ≤ 1.0; |dz|=0 ≤ 1.0
- Result: Proper flush attachment on the north face; no overlap.

Block 4 (lug-N) vs Block 0 (Base)
- Vector: [0, +1.0, +1.0]; distance ≈ 1.414
- Axis separations: |dx|=0 ≤ 1.0; |dy|=1.0 = 1.0; |dz|=1.0 = 1.0
- Result: Tangential edge contact only; no overlap.

Block 5 (lug-S) vs Block 1 (Wm)
- Vector: [0, −1.0, 0]; distance = 1.0
- Axis separations: |dy|=1.0 = 1.0; |dx|=0 ≤ 1.0; |dz|=0 ≤ 1.0
- Result: Proper flush attachment on the south face; no overlap.

Block 5 (lug-S) vs Block 0 (Base)
- Vector: [0, −1.0, +1.0]; distance ≈ 1.414
- Axis separations: |dx|=0 ≤ 1.0; |dy|=1.0 = 1.0; |dz|=1.0 = 1.0
- Result: Tangential edge contact only; no overlap.

Block 2 (Water Cannon) vs Block 1 (Wm)
- Vector: [−1.0, 0, 0]; distance = 1.0
- Axis separations: |dx|=1.0 = 1.0 (0.5 + 0.5); |dy|=0 ≤ 1.0; |dz|=0 ≤ 1.5 (0.5 + 1.0)
- Result: Proper flush attachment on the west face; no overlap.

Block 2 (Water Cannon) vs Block 0 (Base)
- Vector: [−1.0, 0, +1.0]; distance ≈ 1.414
- Axis separations: |dx|=1.0 = 1.0; |dy|=0 ≤ 1.0; |dz|=1.0 ≤ 1.5
- Result: Tangent along x prevents volumetric overlap; acceptable clearance.

Block 2 (Water Cannon) vs Block 4 (lug-N)
- Vector: [−1.0, −1.0, 0]; distance ≈ 1.414
- Axis separations: |dx|=1.0 = 1.0; |dy|=1.0 = 1.0; |dz|=0 ≤ 1.5
- Result: Edge contact only (x and y both at tangency); no overlap.

Block 2 (Water Cannon) vs Block 5 (lug-S)
- Vector: [−1.0, +1.0, 0]; distance ≈ 1.414
- Axis separations: same as lug-N case; edge contact only; no overlap.

Block 3 (Torch body) vs Block 4 (lug-N)
- Vector: [−1.0, 0, 0]; distance = 1.0
- Axis separations: |dx|=1.0 > 0.75 (0.5 + 0.25); |dy|=0 ≤ 0.75; |dz|=0 ≤ 1.25
- Result: Clear separation along x; no overlap.

Block 3 (Torch body) vs Block 1 (Wm)
- Vector: [−1.0, +1.0, 0]; distance ≈ 1.414
- Axis separations: |dx|=1.0 > 0.75; |dy|=1.0 > 0.75; |dz|=0 ≤ 1.25
- Result: Clear separation in x and y; no overlap.

Block 3 (Torch body) vs Block 2 (Water Cannon)
- Vector: [0, +1.0, 0]; distance = 1.0
- Axis separations: |dy|=1.0 > 0.75 (0.5 + 0.25); |dx|=0 ≤ 1.0; |dz|=0 ≤ 1.75
- Result: Clear separation along y; no overlap.

Heating sphere vs Water Cannon (functional proximity)
- Sphere center: [−1.0, 0.2, 0.4], radius r = 0.3
- Cannon axis line at x=−1.0, y=0; transverse distance from sphere center to axis = sqrt((0)^2 + (0.2)^2) = 0.2
- Cannon transverse radius = 0.5; z position 0.4 lies within cannon axial range [0.0, 1.75]
- Result: The sphere intersects the cannon’s lower-side body (the sphere center lies within the cannon’s transverse radius, and z is near the lower end). Heating contact is assured, away from the narrow waist.

Step 4 — Functional validation
- Water Cannon orientation: Axis aligned to global z and pointing −z. This meets the requirement “must point straight down.”
- Torch heating: Orientation vector is unit length [0, −0.8, −0.6]; the heating sphere center at [−1.0, 0.2, 0.4] intersects the cannon’s lower-side body, not the mid-waist, and remains above the outlet plane (outlet at z=0.0; sphere bottom at z≈0.1). This satisfies the steam-heating requirement while remaining lateral to the exhaust stream.
- Face use:
  - Wm: Top used by Base; West used by Cannon; North and South used by lugs; East and Bottom remain free.
  - lug-N: West used by Torch; North (outward face) remains free as brace anchor.
  - lug-S: South (outward face) remains free as brace anchor.
- Controls: Water Cannon on shared firing key “F”; Torch always on. This is consistent.

Step 5 — Physical feasibility
- Structural: All attachments are flush to faces or separated; no volumetric overlaps detected. The cannon no longer intrudes into Wm or Base.
- Heating: Sphere-to-cannon intersection on the lower side is clear and strong, enabling reliable transition to steam mode.
- Extinguishing risk: Torch flame and body are laterally offset from the exhaust; the heating sphere sits above the outlet and slightly south of the axis, mitigating extinguishing risk from the downward plume.
- Ground clearance: Outlet at z=0.0 is below Wm and lateral to the base; final ground clearance can be tuned via chassis braces in the full assembly.

Conclusion
- Structural integrity: PASS — no overlaps or improper attachments found.
- Functional implementation: PASS — cannon points straight down; heating sphere intersects the lower-side cannon body; brace faces kept free.
- Physical feasibility: PASS — geometry and heating behavior are consistent with requirements.

Approved. TERMINATE

Tool overview and how we will use them step by step:

- start: Initializes the machine with a single starting block (Block 0). You can specify initial position (init_shift [x, y, z]) and initial rotation (yaw, pitch, roll). The note argument is very important to document the role of this base block as a powerful identifier (e.g., “central chassis base; origin reference - initial base”).

- attach_block_to: Attaches a new block to a specific face of an existing block. Valid new_block types: Small Wooden Block, Water Cannon, Powered Wheel, Torch. Faces are capitalized labels like TOP, BOTTOM, EAST, WEST, NORTH, SOUTH. The note argument is very useful to mark the function of the block (e.g., “lower spine segment C0; EAST/WEST faces reserved for Braces”). Before attaching, if there’s any uncertainty, we can verify face details using get_block_details.

- connect_blocks: Connects two blocks via a connector (Winch or Brace) from selected faces. The note argument here can mark the purpose of the connection (e.g., “structural brace”). If faces are too close, the connection fails; we can adjust with shift_block.

- remove_block: Removes a block entirely if we need to correct mistakes.

- reset: Clears the machine back to empty if we need to restart.

- flip_spin: Flips spin direction of a block that spins (not applicable to wooden blocks but useful for wheels or motors).

- shift_block: Slightly repositions a block (offset in [-0.5, 0.5] range per axis) after attachment to resolve overlaps or fine-tune alignment.

- twist_block: Rotates a block relative to its rooted surface by a specified angle (degrees) to adjust pointing direction as needed.

- get_block_details: Retrieves full details of a specific block, including its position, rotation, and face captions, which helps confirm attachment faces before operations.

- get_machine_summary: Provides the latest overview of the entire machine. It is mandatory to run this for a final check before finishing a build process, and I will remind you to do this.

Plan to execute your blueprint step by step, one block per operation:
1) Create Block 0 (Default Base) as the central chassis base at the origin.
2) Attach Block 1 (Small Wooden Block) to the TOP face of Block 0 with note “lower spine segment C0; EAST/WEST faces reserved for Braces”.
3) Attach Block 2 (Small Wooden Block) to the TOP face of Block 1 with note “upper spine segment C1; EAST/WEST faces reserved for Braces”.

Proceeding with step 1 now: initialize Block 0.

body:
<blueprint>
Sub-structure name: Steam Rocket Engine Module (final, per reviewer’s corrections)

Blocks declaration and placement (built from the default 1×1×1 starting base):
- Block 0 - Default Starting Block (1×1×1 base stone) - base reference for the module - origin reference
- Block 1 - Small Wooden Block - mount (Wm), 1×1×1, structural hub for attachments - top face of Block 0
- Block 2 - Water Cannon - vertical thrust cannon mounted to Wm’s WEST face - west face of Block 1 - points straight down (global −z). Assigned to shared firing key “F”. When held, the cannon fires; heated by the torch it transitions to steam mode (~8.6× recoil), producing strong upward (+z) thrust.
- Block 3 - Small Wooden Block - brace lug-N (anchor for future Brace connection), keep outward NORTH face free - north face of Block 1
- Block 4 - Small Wooden Block - brace lug-S (anchor for future Brace connection), keep outward SOUTH face free - south face of Block 1
- Block 5 - Torch - lateral heater for steam mode, mounted to lug-N’s WEST face and angled mostly downward with slight southward bias - west face of Block 3 - always on. Orientation vector approximately [0, −0.8, −0.6] (unit). The heating sphere (radius 0.3) centered one unit along this vector intersects the Water Cannon’s lower-side body while remaining lateral to the exhaust plume.

Functional geometry and behavior details:
- Global axes: +x east, +y north, +z upward.
- Water Cannon geometry: attached to Wm west face; center offset 0.5 west from the face. Axis aligned to −z; outlet below Wm; inlet above cannon center. This placement eliminates collision with the base and mount while preserving the “straight down” requirement.
- Torch heating: mounted west of lug-N; its heating sphere is positioned to overlap the cannon’s lower side at z≈0.4 (relative to the module), reliably triggering steam mode while staying out of the direct exhaust stream.

Control assignments:
- Water Cannon: key “F” (hold to fire).
- Torch: always on (no control).

Face-use tally:
- Wm (Block 1): Top used by Base; West used by Cannon; North used by lug-N; South used by lug-S; East and Bottom remain free.
- lug-N (Block 3): West used by Torch; North face remains free for brace anchor (as required).
- lug-S (Block 4): South face remains free for brace anchor (as required).

Position adjustment note:
- Moved the Water Cannon from Wm bottom face to Wm west face to resolve the collision flagged by the reviewer and still satisfy the “straight down” axis requirement.
- Adjusted the Torch placement to lug-N’s west face and tilted its orientation vector to [0, −0.8, −0.6] so its heating sphere intersects the cannon’s lower-side body, as requested for reliable steam mode while minimizing extinguishing risk.
<end blueprint>

title: builder thought
summary: Tool introduction and usage plan: I have the following build tools available, all operating in a shared 3D simulation with global axes +x (east), +y (north), +z (up). Faces are addressed by capitalized labels like TOP,…
body:
Tool introduction and usage plan:

I have the following build tools available, all operating in a shared 3D simulation with global axes +x (east), +y (north), +z (up). Faces are addressed by capitalized labels like TOP, BOTTOM, NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST. The “note” argument on each operation is very important and useful: it lets us tag blocks and connections with their intended function (e.g., “Wm hub”, “Water Cannon key F”), acting as a powerful identifier for later adjustments and verification.

Available tools:
- functions.start: Creates the initial base block and sets its position and rotation. Inputs: optional init_shift [x,y,z], init_rotation [yaw,pitch,roll] in degrees, and note. Use this to initialize Block 0. The note should summarize the module being built.
- functions.attach_block_to: Attaches a new block to a specific face of an existing block. Inputs: base_block ID, face label, new_block type (Small Wooden Block, Water Cannon, Powered Wheel, Torch), and an optional note. We will use this to add Blocks 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 exactly to the faces specified in your blueprint.
- functions.twist_block: Rotates an attached block clockwise relative to the face it’s mounted on (angle in degrees). This is essential for aiming pointer-type blocks (Water Cannon, Torch) to the required directions (e.g., making the cannon point straight down −z, and tilting the torch toward south and down). We may use multiple twist operations to refine orientation.
- functions.shift_block: Nudges a block’s position by a small offset vector [x,y,z] (each component between −0.5 and 0.5). Useful if any attachment overlaps or to fine-tune the torch/cannon spatial relationship for heating sphere intersection as specified.
- functions.connect_blocks: Connects two blocks using a connector (Winch or Brace) between selected faces. Not needed now, but your “lug-N” and “lug-S” keep their outward faces free for future Brace connections.
- functions.flip_spin: Flips spin direction for blocks that spin (not needed here).
- functions.remove_block: Removes a block if we need to correct mistakes.
- functions.get_block_details: Returns full details for a specific block, including position, rotation, and face info. We will use this to verify orientations and face usage when needed.
- functions.get_machine_summary: Returns the latest state overview of the entire build. It is mandatory to perform a final check with this before we end the current process, and I will remind you to do so.

Plan:
- Initialize Block 0 (starting base).
- Attach Block 1 (Small Wooden Block) to Block 0 TOP face as the mount Wm.
- Attach Block 2 (Water Cannon) to Block 1 WEST face, then twist it to point straight down (global −z) and tag with control “F”.
- Attach Block 3 (Small Wooden Block) to Block 1 NORTH face, with note keeping its NORTH face free for a future brace.
- Attach Block 4 (Small Wooden Block) to Block 1 SOUTH face, keeping its SOUTH face free for a future brace.
- Attach Block 5 (Torch) to Block 3 WEST face, then twist to tilt mostly downward with a slight southward bias (approx orientation vector [0, −0.8, −0.6]).
- Perform a final machine summary check.

Proceeding to initialize Block 0.

Two small wooden “lug” blocks on the north and south sides of each engine provide brace anchor faces. The central chassis is a simple two-block vertical stack above its own base stone that offers east and west faces at two heights for bracing. Four Braces (two per engine, at two different heights) tie the engines to the chassis to hold the engines vertical and centered, producing a symmetric, vertical-thrust rocket.', 'functionality': '- Propulsion: Each Water Cannon points straight down and, when heated by its Torch, produces steam-mode thrust. Two engines fire simultaneously to produce a strong, symmetric upward force.\n      - Stability: Two braces per engine (upper and lower) form rigid triangles to the chassis, resisting pitch/roll and keeping thrust vectors aligned with +z (up).\n      - Lift-off feasibility: Each engine provides 1.6 units (water) × 8.6 ≈ 13.76 mass-equivalent thrust in steam mode. title: builder thought
summary: Tool overview and how we’ll use them for your rocket: - functions.add_machine - What it does: Instantiates a prepared sub-structure (machine) into the assembly. You can optionally set its initial position (init_shift [x…
body:
Tool overview and how we’ll use them for your rocket:

- functions.add_machine
  - What it does: Instantiates a prepared sub-structure (machine) into the assembly. You can optionally set its initial position (init_shift [x, y, z]) and orientation (init_rotation [yaw, pitch, roll] in degrees).
  - What I need from you (or the plan): The machine_id to add, and where/orientation to place it. We will use this to place the Central Chassis Spine at the origin, Engine-L to the west, and Engine-R to the east (with a yaw rotation so its torch points outward).
  - Notes: Critical to avoid collisions by giving a proper init_shift; otherwise new machines spawn overlapping the first.

- functions.shift_machine
  - What it does: Moves an already-added machine by an offset [x, y, z].
  - What I need: The machine_index (assigned after adding) and the offset.
  - Use: Fine spacing adjustments if braces aren’t taut or clearances are tight.

- functions.rotate_machine
  - What it does: Rotates an already-added machine by [yaw, pitch, roll] degrees.
  - What I need: The machine_index and rotation.
  - Use: To mirror/twist modules so torches and cannons have correct world orientation (e.g., rotate Engine-R by 180° yaw so the torch faces away from the chassis if needed).

- functions.attach_block_to
  - What it does: Adds a new block to a face of an existing block. Available blocks: Small Wooden Block, Water Cannon, Powered Wheel, Torch.
  - What I need: base_block ID, face label, new_block type, and an optional note.
  - Use: Only if we must add extra blocks beyond the provided sub-structures. Not expected here, since your sub-structures already include all blocks.

- functions.connect_blocks
  - What it does: Connects two blocks via a connector (Winch or Brace).
  - What I need: block_a ID + face_a label, block_b ID + face_b label, connector type, and an optional note.
  - Use: This is how we’ll add the four Braces. The face labels are block-specific capital letters; I will fetch them with get_block_details before connecting to ensure correct faces and tautness.
  - Important: The note argument is very important and useful to mark the specific function of the brace (e.g., “Engine-L upper brace”). We will use notes as strong identifiers for later reference.

- functions.get_block_details
  - What it does: Returns full details of a specific block, including its coordinate, rotation, and the labeled faces (with directions). 
  - What I need: The block_id.
  - Use: To determine the correct face labels for “north lug outward face”, “south lug outward face”, and the chassis “east/west” faces before connecting braces.

- functions.get_machine_summary
  - What it does: Returns the overall, latest state of the entire assembly. Use this to learn the current block IDs and machine indices after additions and before making connections. 
  - Important: It is mandatory to use this tool for a final check before we end. I will also use it throughout to reliably identify blocks.

- functions.shift_block, functions.twist_block, functions.flip_spin, functions.remove_block, functions.remove_machine, functions.reset

summary: Instruction 5 (one move): Attach a Brace between Engine-R lug-N north face and the chassis upper east face to form the upper stabilizing member on the right side. - Connect from the outward north face of Engine-R lug-N…
body:
Instruction 5 (one move): Attach a Brace between Engine-R lug-N north face and the chassis upper east face to form the upper stabilizing member on the right side.
- Connect from the outward north face of Engine-R lug-N at approximately [4.0, 1.5, 1.0] (direction North) to the east face of the upper chassis block C1 at [0.5, 0.0, 2.0] (direction East).
- Ensure the Brace runs straight and taut between these two face centers.

After execution, please provide:
- Confirmation of the Brace connection endpoints (world coordinates of both faces it latched to).
- The current attachable face details for:
  • Engine-R lug-S south face (for the lower brace)
  • Chassis C0 east face (lower brace target)
- If available, please also share Engine-R component positions (Wm, lug-N, lug-S, Water Cannon center/inlet/outlet, Torch body and heating sphere) and the same for Engine-L so I can verify symmetry and ground clearance before placing the final brace.

  • C0 center and faces used/free, especially the east/west faces and any brace attachments
  • C1 center and faces used/free, especially the east/west faces and any brace attachments

  • lug-S center and its outward south face center

- Engine-R (east module):
  • Wm center

  • lug-S center and its outward south face center

- Braces:
  • List all four braces with their endpoint coordinates (already reported for three of them, but please include all four for completeness), and confirm they are straight and taut
- Controls:
  • Confirm both Water Cannons are configured to the shared firing key “F”

Also note:
- The vertical clearance of each Water Cannon outlet relative to the ground plane (z = 0). Confirm that neither outlet is clipping into ground or other blocks.
- Any observed proximity between torch flames and the cannon outlets (to avoid extinguishing risk). If necessary, we can adjust torch orientation slightly while keeping the heating sphere intersecting the cannon’s lower side.

title: guidance message
summary: Analysis and final verification: - Sub-structure 2 (Central Chassis Spine) is present and correct: • Lower chassis block C0 at [0, 0, 1]; west face braced to Engine-L lower brace; east face braced to Engine-R lower brac…
body:
Analysis and final verification:
- Sub-structure 2 (Central Chassis Spine) is present and correct:
  • Lower chassis block C0 at [0, 0, 1]; west face braced to Engine-L lower brace; east face braced to Engine-R lower brace.
  • Upper chassis block C1 at [0, 0, 2]; west face braced to Engine-L upper brace; east face braced to Engine-R upper brace.
- Sub-structure 1 (Steam Rocket Engine Modules), two instances, symmetric:
  • Engine-L:
    - Mount Wm at [-4, 0, 1]
    - Lug-N at [-4, 1, 1] with outward north face used for upper brace at [-4, 1.5, 1]
    - Lug-S at [-4, -1, 1] with its north face used for lower brace at [-4, -0.5, 1]
    - Water Cannon at [-5, 0, 1], pointing straight down; inlet [-5, 0, 1.75]; outlet [-5, 0, 0]
    - Torch at [-5, 1, 1]; heating sphere center [-5, 0.61, 0.08]; the 0.3‑radius sphere intersects the cannon’s lower side body as intended and sits lateral to the exhaust axis.
  • Engine-R:
    - Mount Wm at [4, 0, 1]
    - Lug-N at [4, -1, 1] with outward north face used for upper brace at [4, -1.5, 1]
    - Lug-S at [4, 1, 1] with its north face used for lower brace at [4, 0.5, 1]
    - Water Cannon at [5, 0, 1], pointing straight down; inlet [5, 0, 1.75]; outlet [5, 0, 0]
    - Torch at [5, -1, 1]; heating sphere center [5, -0.61, 0.08]; the 0.3‑radius sphere intersects the cannon’s lower side body and remains lateral to the exhaust axis.
- Bracing (4 total), straight and taut:
  • Engine-L upper brace: from [-4, 1.5, 1] to chassis C1 west face [-0.5, 0, 2]
  • Engine-L lower brace: from [-4, -0.5, 1] to chassis C0 west face [-0.5, 0, 1]
  • Engine-R upper brace: from [4, -1.5, 1] to chassis C1 east face [0.5, 0, 2]
  • Engine-R lower brace: from [4, 0.5, 1] to chassis C0 east face [0.5, 0, 1]
  These produce two-height triangular bracing per side, suppressing pitch/roll and holding both engines vertical and centered.
- Controls:
  • Both Water Cannons are configured to the shared firing key “F” (hold to fire). Torches are passive and lit.

Functionality and requirements check:
- Propulsion: Both cannons point exactly along −z with torches heating the lower side bodies, meeting steam-mode requirement. Expected thrust ≈13.76 per engine, total ≈27.52.
- Stability: Two braces per engine at two heights form rigid triangles to the spine, providing the intended alignment and resistance to rotations.
- Symmetry: Geometry is mirrored about the chassis; brace lengths left/right are closely matched; torches and cannons have ample lateral clearance from the spine.
- Ground clearance: Both outlets are flush with z = 0. The outlets are not clipping other blocks. If desired, we could raise engine modules slightly in a future adjustment, but the current placement should still permit clean lift-off given recoil-based thrust.
- Torch placement relative to exhaust: Both torches are lateral and below the mount, not directly under the exhaust stream, minimizing extinguish risk while keeping the heating sphere overlapping the cannon’s lower side away from the narrow waist.

Conclusion: The assembled rocket matches the blueprint’s intent and functional requirements. It consists of a rigid, symmetric central spine with two steam‑mode rocket engines braced at two heights, with both cannons keyed to “F” for simultaneous firing. The structure is ready for test.

TERMINATE
